summary: I come from Afghanistan, where life became too dangerous for me and my family. The Taliban have controlled much of the country since 1994, causing terror and violence against civilians like us. My family suffered greatly there, with my father working as a farmer and me helping him until we had to flee for our safety. Sadly, my father was killed by the Taliban, and my younger brother was kidnapped. Leaving Afghanistan was painful, but necessary for my survival.

I am originally from Kunduz, a city in the north of Afghanistan. It was there that I lived with my two brothers and four sisters, and where my father worked as a farmer. The situation became so dangerous in Kunduz that I had to stop going to school after three years. Our family faced direct threats, especially after my older brother's relationship with a girl whose family was Taliban. Eventually, we had to flee Kunduz to save our lives.

The Taliban have been a constant threat in Afghanistan since 1994, fighting against the government and terrorizing the population. They impose their own harsh laws, abduct, extort, and kill people, including members of my family. My older brother was severely punished by them because he secretly met a girl from a Taliban family. Later, my father was killed by the Taliban, and my younger brother was kidnapped. Their presence forced my family and me to flee our home and seek safety abroad.

After fleeing Afghanistan, I managed to cross the border into Turkey, although my family was sent back. I was sixteen at the time and had to make difficult decisions with my father's support to continue on to Europe for safety. In Turkey, I faced challenges at the border police and had to travel onward by boat to Greece. Turkey was a crucial but difficult stop in my journey to find refuge and a new life.

From Turkey, I traveled by a small, overcrowded boat to Greece as part of my escape route to Europe. This was a dangerous and frightening part of my journey, but it was necessary to reach safety. After arriving in Greece, I continued my journey by bus, train, and on foot toward Germany. Greece was a brief but critical passage on my path to a new life.

I arrived in Germany after a long and difficult journey through Turkey and Greece. At first, I stayed in a first reception center before moving to a youth facility in Cottbus. When I turned eighteen, I got my own apartment and started to rebuild my life. Germany gave me safety and the chance to go back to school, now in the 10th grade, and to plan for training. Although I face challenges like feeling like a foreigner, I am grateful to live here in peace.

In Germany, I was placed in a youth facility in Cottbus after arriving from the first reception center. This was an important step in my adjustment to life here, providing me with support and stability. Living in Cottbus helped me start to recover from the trauma of my past and focus on my education and future goals. It was a place where I began to feel a bit more secure despite the difficulties.
